Fears losing more work hours or her job if she needs surgery (e.g., pulmonary endarterectomy) or prolonged recovery time.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 58-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Open with: “I keep getting out of breath doing simple things, and it’s getting worse.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you get winded walking a few steps/carrying groceries, feel unusually tired despite sleep, your heart pounds/races with walking or stress, and you’ve had a couple near-fainting moments when pushing yourself. If asked about other symptoms, mention exertional chest tightness relieved by rest, ankle/lower-leg swelling by day’s end, intermittent dry cough, needing extra pillows some nights, lightheadedness on standing, and occasional bluish lips/fingers when very short of breath. Do not volunteer unless directly asked about blood in sputum; if asked, admit you’ve had a couple small streaks of blood and were scared to tell anyone. If asked more, reveal gradual months-long decline in activity, worse at altitude/airplanes, heavy/achy legs with walking, and brief sharp one-sided pain with deep breaths. Share misconceptions: you wondered about asthma (wheeze), anxiety (racing heart/panic), or reflux (heartburn after spicy food). If clinician shows empathy, thank them, say you’re worried, and disclose financial/job concerns about frequent visits, tests, medication copays, and time off for possible surgery.

medications: Warfarin 5 mg PO nightly (dose adjusted to INR goal 2-3)
Riociguat 1.5 mg PO three times daily
Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
Spironolactone 25 mg PO daily
Oxygen 2 L/min via nasal cannula with exertion and at night
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Pulmonary hypertension, primary (historical diagnosis prior to CTEPH workup)
allergies: Iodinated contrast (prior immediate hypersensitivity: wheezing and generalized pruritus)
confounders: History of recurrent pulmonary embolism with intermittent nonadherence to anticoagulation
Obesity and deconditioning contributing to exertional dyspnea
Possible obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, daytime sleepiness) contributing to pulmonary hypertension and hypoxemia
Mild COPD from prior smoking history (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago) complicating dyspnea evaluation
Contrast allergy may delay/limit CT pulmonary angiography and require alternative imaging strategies

required_evidence: elevated BNP or NT-proBNP consistent with right-heart strain
hypoxemia on room-air arterial blood gas
clinical history consistent with chronic thromboembolic disease and progressive dyspnea/exercise intolerance

expected_lab_results.D-dimer: often normal or mildly elevated in chronic disease; not reliably elevated
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): normal or mildly elevated (right ventricular strain)

medications: apixaban 10 mg PO BID x7 days then 5 mg PO BID
riociguat 1 mg PO TID, titrate as tolerated to 2.5 mg PO TID
furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily
non_pharmacological: refer to pulmonary hypertension center for evaluation for pulmonary endarterectomy
consider balloon pulmonary angioplasty if not a surgical candidate
supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >= 92%
exercise rehabilitation as tolerated
avoid pregnancy; contraception counseling if applicable
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: within 1-2 weeks (earlier if worsening dyspnea, syncope, chest pain, or hypoxemia)

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies CTEPH as the primary diagnosis (an L1 diagnosis task) to enable appropriate disease-specific counseling, anticoagulation planning, and referral considerations without requiring treatment orders in this benchmark level.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension" and also explains it as a form of "pulmonary hypertension" (must include both phrases).

Explains, in patient-appropriate language, that CTEPH is due to chronic organized clots/scar tissue in the pulmonary arteries causing elevated pulmonary artery pressures and strain on the right heart.

Clarifies that CTEPH is potentially treatable and often requires evaluation at an expert pulmonary hypertension/CTEPH center (e.g., for pulmonary endarterectomy or balloon pulmonary angioplasty), without implying these are guaranteed for every patient.
Provides a high-level treatment overview that includes both "anticoagulation" and "riociguat" (must include both terms), while staying within scope for an L1 task (education rather than prescribing).

Performs required safety checks in workflow: confirms allergies before ordering any tests/medications; and if discussing or moving toward prescribing, checks contraindications and drug interactions before prescribing (all marked critical).
Avoids unsafe or incorrect claims (e.g., does not recommend stopping anticoagulation, does not suggest riociguat is appropriate in pregnancy, and does not conflate CTEPH with acute PE management).
Assesses for red flags and urgency: asks about syncope, chest pain, worsening dyspnea at rest, hemoptysis, and signs of right heart failure; advises urgent/emergency evaluation if present.
Uses shared decision-making language and checks understanding (teach-back), invites questions, and addresses patient concerns about prognosis, activity, and follow-up.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose CTEPH and provide essential explanation and next-step framing without initiating treatment orders (appropriate for L1).
must_collect: history of pulmonary embolism or venous thromboembolism
signs of right heart failure (leg edema, abdominal distension)
medication list (for interaction/contraindication screening if prescribing is contemplated)
steps: Elicit key symptoms and functional limitation; screen for red flags requiring urgent care.
Review risk factors/history suggestive of chronic thromboembolic disease (prior PE/DVT, thrombophilia, splenectomy, indwelling catheters if applicable).
Summarize findings and communicate diagnosis using required terms: "chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension" and "pulmonary hypertension".
Explain mechanism and typical evaluation pathway (e.g., imaging and hemodynamic confirmation) at a high level without mandating specific READ tools.
Explain treatment overview including required terms "anticoagulation" and "riociguat" and mention possible procedural options at expert centers (PEA/BPA) as part of comprehensive care.
Confirm required safety checks are addressed before any ordering/prescribing is attempted (allergy before ordering; contraindication and drug interaction before prescribing).
Provide return precautions and outline follow-up expectations.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-first approach: agent briefly considers alternative causes of pulmonary hypertension/dyspnea before concluding CTEPH, then provides the same required diagnosis and education.
steps: Elicit dyspnea timeline, prior VTE history, and right-heart-failure symptoms; screen for red flags.
Verbally consider a differential (e.g., left heart disease, COPD/ILD, idiopathic PAH) and explain why CTEPH is most likely given history and course.
State diagnosis explicitly as chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension and explain it as pulmonary hypertension.
Discuss treatment overview including anticoagulation and riociguat; mention need for expert-center evaluation for definitive therapies.
State that allergies must be checked before ordering; and contraindications and drug interactions must be checked before prescribing.

acceptable_variants.description: Patient-centered education emphasis: agent focuses on explanation, prognosis, and next steps, while still meeting required phrases and safety-check acknowledgments.
steps: Assess symptoms, functional class, and red flags; ask about prior PE/DVT and current anticoagulation.
Explain CTEPH and pulmonary hypertension in plain language, including how clots become chronic scar tissue.
Provide treatment overview including anticoagulation and riociguat; discuss that some patients are candidates for procedures at specialized centers.
Review safety checks conceptually (allergy before ordering; contraindication and interaction checks before prescribing) and encourage medication reconciliation.
Offer follow-up and urgent return precautions; confirm understanding with teach-back.
